title: Semantic Geometry
concept: Embedding spaces have geometry: distance can reflect how related two items are, while directions can capture patterns like category shifts or role changes, so meaning is stored not just in points but in spatial relationships.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In an embedding space, what usually suggests that two items are related?

options.text: Their coordinates land near each other.
Their names start with the same letter.
They were assigned consecutive integer IDs.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Now we can go one level deeper: not just where points are, but what distance and direction between them can mean.

cards.title: The arrows matter, not just the dots
cards.body: On a map, knowing that two towns are close is useful. But the arrow from one town to another also tells you something: north or south, mountain or coast, short trip or long trip. Embedding spaces can carry that same extra layer of structure.

cards.title: Meaning lives in distances and directions
cards.body: The term semantic geometry means that an embedding space is not just a bag of coordinates. The distance between points can reflect how similar they are, while the direction from one point to another can reflect a consistent relationship. The geometry itself becomes part of the model's knowledge.

cards.title: Walking across a themed museum
cards.body: Imagine a museum where one wing contains animals, another contains vehicles, and another contains tools. A short walk often means a similar theme. A walk in a certain direction may move you from baby forms to adult forms, or from old technology to modern technology. Embedding geometry works in that spirit.

cards.title: The same relationship can point the same way
cards.body: Suppose the arrow from dog to puppy points toward “younger version.” If the space is well organized, the arrow from cat to kitten may point in a similar direction. The model is not memorizing only individual words. It is storing patterns of relation inside the geometry.

cards.title: Embedding space is not a literal physical map
cards.body: A common myth is that semantic geometry means there is a perfect human-drawn atlas hiding in the model. Not quite. The coordinates are learned for the model's task, and different trainings can rotate or stretch the space. What matters is the pattern of relationships, not whether one axis is literally “north.”

cards.title: Closeness tells one story; direction tells another
cards.body: Two geometric questions can reveal two different parts of meaning:

left.text:
How near are two items?
Often reflects similarity or shared context
Good for grouping neighbors

right.text:
How do you move from one item to another?
Can capture relationship patterns
Good for analogy-like structure

options.text: The layout of the embedding space itself carries meaning through distances and directions.
Only the item names matter; coordinates are irrelevant once training ends.
All embeddings must live on a two-dimensional sheet.
Every coordinate axis must match a school geometry label like x or y.

exercises.explain: Correct. The point is that relationships in the space can encode useful patterns, not just the raw coordinates themselves.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In embedding spaces, distance can reflect similarity while direction can reflect the type of relationship between items.
exercises.explain: True. That is the core intuition behind semantic geometry.

exercises.sentence: In semantic geometry, ___ often reflects how similar two embeddings are.
exercises.explain: Exactly. Nearby points often signal related meaning or behavior.

exercises.prompt: Which observation best matches semantic geometry?

options.text: The move from “cat” to “kitten” resembles the move from “dog” to “puppy,” so the relationship pattern appears in the space.
Words can be related only if they were typed next to each other in a spreadsheet.
Two embeddings are meaningful only if humans can name every coordinate directly.

exercises.explain: Right. Semantic geometry is about relation patterns emerging from the learned layout.

exercises.prompt: A researcher notices that the shift from “intern” to “manager” resembles the shift from “student” to “teacher” inside an embedding space. What does that suggest?

options.text: The embedding geometry may be encoding a reusable relationship direction, not just isolated item identities.
The embeddings have failed because related items should never move in similar directions.
Only the absolute size of one coordinate matters in a semantic space.
Embedding models cannot represent similarity and role changes at the same time.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Similar arrows can hint that the space has learned a relation pattern, not just a list of nearby points.
